coredata.dc:title: Transport properties of graphene quantum dots in glycerol and distilled water
coredata.dc:creator: Goharshadi, Elaheh K.
Niyazi, Zohreh
Shafaee, Masoomeh
Moghaddam, Monireh B.
Ludwig, Ralf
Namayandeh-Jorabchi, Majid
coredata.dc:description: In this work, for the first time, we measured the transport properties including rheology, thermal conductivity (TC), and electrical conductivity (EC) of the nanofluids of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in glycerol and distilled water at various mass fractions and temperatures. The GQDs were prepared with the average particle size of 20nm by one-step green pyrolysis of citric acid. The GQDs-glycerol suspensions behaved as shear-thinning or pseudoplastic fluids at low shear rates and temperatures. The viscosity of glycerol decreases by loading GQDs so that 49.97% reduction in viscosity of glycerol was achieved by loading of 2% GQDs for shear rate of 0.66s−1 at 20°C. The TC of distilled water increases about 9% when 2% GQDs were loaded at 298K. Also, the TC of GQDs-glycerol suspensions increased exponentially with raising temperature. The EC of distilled water increases by loading GQDs through the formation of surface charge by polarization of GQDs in distilled water. The maximum EC enhancement for the nanofluid with mass fraction of 0.0004 was around 14,342% at 25°C.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Nanofluid
Rheology
Thermal conductivity
Electrical conductivity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) XRD pattern b) FTIR spectrum c) Raman spectrum d) UV–vis spectrum e) TEM image (f) the PSD (g) AFM topography image with the width and height profile along the line in the image of GQDs.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra (b) fluorescence images of GQDs.
(a) The digital photo of nanofluids of GQDs in distilled water and glycerol (b) the zeta potential of nanofluids of GQDS in distilled water.
The logarithm of viscosity as a function of shear rate for glycerol at different temperatures.
The logarithm of viscosity of glycerol and GQDs–glycerol suspensions as a function of shear rate at different mass fractions at 303.15K.
The logarithm of shear stress versus shear rate for glycerol and GQDs–glycerol suspensions for different mass fractions at 303.15K.

The logarithm of viscosity versus shear rate for GQDS–glycerol suspensions at (a) f
m
=0.0025 and (b) f
m
=0.0200 at different temperatures.

(a) TC (b) TC enhancement% of GQDs-distilled water as a function of mass fraction at 298K (c) TC of GQDs-distilled water as a function of temperature for f
m
=0.015.
(a) EC (b) EC enhancement of GQDs-distilled water as a function of mass fraction at different temperatures.
The decrease in viscosity of the GQDs–glycerol suspensions at different temperatures and mass fractions at constant shear rate.
The parameters of Eq. (5) and the correlation coefficients for GQDs-distilled water nanofluids at the studied temperatures.

head.ce:title: Transport properties of graphene quantum dots in glycerol and distilled water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad 9177948974, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Nano Research, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad 9177948974, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Physical and Theoretical Chemistry, University of Rostock, Dr.-Lorenz-Weg 1, D-18059 Rostock, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemistry, Physical and Theoretical Chemistry

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad 9177948974, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, for the first time, we measured the transport properties including rheology, thermal conductivity (TC), and electrical conductivity (EC) of the nanofluids of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in glycerol and distilled water at various mass fractions and temperatures. The GQDs were prepared with the average particle size of 20nm by one-step green pyrolysis of citric acid. The GQDs-glycerol suspensions behaved as shear-thinning or pseudoplastic fluids at low shear rates and temperatures. The viscosity of glycerol decreases by loading GQDs so that 49.97% reduction in viscosity of glycerol was achieved by loading of 2% GQDs for shear rate of 0.66s−1 at 20°C. The TC of distilled water increases about 9% when 2% GQDs were loaded at 298K. Also, the TC of GQDs-glycerol suspensions increased exponentially with raising temperature. The EC of distilled water increases by loading GQDs through the formation of surface charge by polarization of GQDs in distilled water. The maximum EC enhancement for the nanofluid with mass fraction of 0.0004 was around 14,342% at 25°C.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanofluids, dispersions of nano-size particles in base fluids, have several promising industrial and medical applications [1]. Most of nanofluids possess improved thermal transport properties such as thermal conductivity (TC) and viscosity compared with their corresponding base fluids [2]. In fact, nanofluids have potential to be considered as the smart next-generation heat transfer fluids.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), 0 D quantum-confined graphene, are graphene nanosheets of smaller than 100nm. Compared with conventional QDs such as metal oxides or metal sulfides, they have attracted much attention due to their low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, excellent solubility, high stability, stable photoluminescence, good resistance to photobleaching, high electrical conductivity, and high thermal conductivity [3,4]. The excellent properties of GQDs have triggered them for various potential applications including fuel cells [5], photovoltaic devices [6], optoelectronic devices [7], catalysis [8], biological imaging [9,10], electroluminescence as light-emitting diodes [7], etc.

Both top-down and bottom-up methods have been used to fabricate GQDs. Preparing of GQDs by top-down methods involves cutting of large carbonaceous materials by various techniques like hydrothermal [9], electron beam lithography [11], electrochemical etching of a graphene electrode [12], and ultrasonication breaking of graphene [13,14]. The expensive starting materials, long treatment times, low yield, low solubility in aqueous solutions, and harsh conditions including high temperature and use of volatile organic solvents, limit most of these methods. Small carbonaceous precursors including citric acid or glucose are used in bottom-up approaches to prepare GQDS with controllable morphologies [15]. However, these methods frequently suffer from both time-consuming and use of a strong acid or an alkali. Recently, a rapid bottom-up method was developed to prepare GQDs by pyrolysis of carbonaceous materials like citric acid [16].

Herein, in continuation of our previous works on measuring the transport properties of the nanofluids of carbonaceous nanomaterials [17–20], we want to report the results of measurements of transport properties of the nanofluids of GQDs in distilled water and glycerol. Recently, Rasheed et al. [21] reviewed the investigations on graphene-based nanofluids and nanolubricants.

To the best of our knowledge, the transport properties of nanofluids of GQDs have not been reported yet. The goals of the present work are to fabricate the nanofluids of GQDs in distilled water and glycerol and measure the TC, rheological properties, and electrical conductivity (EC) of these nanofluids as a function of both mass fraction and temperature.

Citric acid (CA) (99.5%), NaOH (≥97%), and glycerol (98%) were purchased from Fluka Company. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Distilled water used throughout the experiments.

The GQDs were prepared by a one-step pyrolysis of a weak organic tribasic acid using a heating mantle device [16]. Briefly, 0.01mol CA was heated for about 5min. First, the CA was liquated and then color of the liquid changed from colorless to orange. The solution was cooled to room temperature. Then, 100mL of 10mgmL−1 NaOH solution was added dropwise to neutralize the solution. The solution was first dialyzed in a dialysis bag of 3500Da molecular weight for 3–4days. Finally, the excess amount of solvent was removed by freeze-drying.

The Electrothermal MY6404 Heating Mantle (200W) was used for pyrolysis of citric acid. The freeze-drying instrument (ALPHA 1-2 LD PLUS) was used for removal excess amount of solvent in the synthesis of GQDs. The zeta potential of GQDS–distilled water suspension as a function of pH was measured by the Zeta sizer (Nano-ZS) from Malvern instrument.

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern of GQDs was determined by means of a Bruker/D8 Advanced diffractometer in the 2θ range from 5° to 80°, by 0.04° steps, using Cu Kα (λ=0.15406nm) radiation. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of prepared GQDs was recorded at room temperature with a KBr pellet on a Thermo Nicolet-Avatar 370 Spectrometer. The Raman spectrum of GQDs was measured by handheld Raman analyzer (RIGAKU, ™FIRSTGUARD). The UV–vis absorbance spectrum of GQDs was recorded using a Unico UV-2100 spectrophotometer at room temperature. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of GQDs was taken using a LEO 912 AB instrument. The electron beam accelerating voltage was 120kV. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) image of GQDs deposited on a mica substrate was obtained by AraResearch (model no. 0101/A, Iran) at ambient temperature using nanoprobe cantilever in non-contact mode.
The luminescence spectrometer RF-1501, Shimadzu and fluorescence microscopic Olympus-DP71, BX51 were used for obtaining the fluorescence properties of prepared GQDs.

The rheological properties of the suspensions of GQDs in glycerol were measured using a Brookfield viscometer (LV DV-II+Pro EXTRA) with a small sample adaptor. The adaptor consists of a cylindrical sample holder, a water jacket, and spindle. The TC was measured using the KD2 Pro Thermal Properties Analyzer (Decagon Devises, Inc.). KD2 Pro is based on the transient hot wire method. The sample container was connected to a circulating water bath (Thermo Haake K10) to ensure the measurements are performed at constant temperature.

The EC of the nanofluids was measured using an EDT instrument BA 380 (DC 9V 0.5W) with accuracy of ±1%. The measurements were performed in the range of 0.1μS/cm–200mS/cm. The measuring cell was connected to a circulating cooling water bath (BL 7100, Major Science) to control the water temperature.
The prepared GQDs were dispersed in 20mL distilled water or glycerol with different mass fractions with the help of a sonicator (Sonicator 4000). No dispersant/surfactant was used in the formulation.

Fig. 1a shows the XRD pattern of GQDs. It represents a (002) peak centered at 2θ=32°. The broad nature of this peak is due to the small size of GQDs. The broadness is due to small size of GQDs. The interlayer spacing was calculated as 0.29nm using Bragg equation:
(1)
2 d sin θ = nλ
where θ is the Bragg angle, d is interlayer spacing, n is a positive integer, and λ is X-ray wavelength CuKα.

The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1b) reveals the presence of some oxygen functional groups on the surfaces and edges of GQDs. Fig. 1b represents a broad peak around 3426cm−1 which is attributed to the bending vibration of OH group. The peaks at 1582.49cm−1 and 1389.45cm−1 originate from CC and COO
bonds which confirm the presence of the COOH group. The peak at 1079.70cm−1 is associated with COH bond which confirms the presence of the hydroxyl groups. The peak at 1729cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of CO bond of carbonyl groups. The peak at 2964cm−1 originates from the CH bond. The presence of the oxygen-containing functional groups such as carboxylic acid, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups makes the GQDs be well soluble in water.

Raman spectroscopy is a powerful and non-destructive technique for characterizing the graphitic materials [22]. The Raman spectrum of GQDs is shown in Fig. 1c. There are two distinctive peaks assigned to D band (1408cm−1) and G band (1640cm−1). The D band with A1g symmetry is attributed to the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds [19]. The G band is due to the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon atoms which is a doubly degenerate phonon mode with E
2g symmetry. This appears at 1575cm−1 for graphite. This blue shift (1640 instead of 1575cm−1) may be related to the oxidation of graphite. The relative intensity of the D band to the G-band (ID/IG) was calculated as 0.73 which is a measure of the defects.

Fig. 1d shows the UV–vis spectrum of GQDs dispersed in water. The peak at around 228nm is attributed to π → π* transition of aromatic CC bonds. The band gap energy, E
g , was calculated from UV–Vis absorption data using Tauc's equation [19]:
(2)
α hν
n
= B
hν −
E g
where α, hν, and B are absorption coefficient, photon energy, and a constant relative to the material, respectively. The parameter n represents the nature of optical transition, i.e. n =2 for direct transitions and n =1/2 for indirect transitions. The optical band gap can be obtained by plotting (αhν)1/2 against the photon energy and extrapolating the linear part of the plot to the x-axis (the inset of Fig. 1d). The estimated band gap of GQDs was calculated as 4.63eV. Although graphene is a zero band gap semiconductor, GQDs is non-zero band gap semiconductor because of quantum confinements [23].

The morphology of GQDs was investigated by transition electron microscopy (TEM). As Fig. 1e shows, the particles are well dispersed and homogenized. The particle size distribution (PSD) histogram was obtained by Digimizer software by choosing 105 particles in TEM image. The PSD histogram in Fig. 1f shows the GQDs have an average particle size of 20nm with a narrow size distribution.
The AFM image/height profile of prepared GQDs is presented in Fig. 1g. As the inset of this figure shows the GQDs consist the nanosheets with maximum ∼12nm in height.

One of the important features of the GQDs is their fluorescence properties [24] which are investigated using both spectroscopy and the microscopy. When the prepared GQDs were excited at wavelengths of 320, 350, and 400nm, the fluorescence peaks were emitted at 450, 470, 490nm, respectively (Fig. 2a). Also, the fluorescence properties of GQDs were investigated using a fluorescence microscope. Upon irradiation with violet (330–385nm), blue (460–500nm), and green (535–585nm) lights, the GQDs emitted blue, green, and red fluorescence, respectively (Fig. 2b–d).

The nanofluids of GQDs in distilled water and glycerol were stable at least for two months. Fig. 3a shows the digital photo of GQDs in distilled water and glycerol when the mass fraction of GQDs was 0.02 after two months. To investigate the stability of GQDs in distilled water, the zeta potential of GQDs-distilled water nanofluid as a function of pH was measured and shown in Fig. 3b. As this figure shows, the nanofluid has a zeta potential more than −40mV when the pH is equal or greater than to 7. Hence, the nanofluids of GQDs-distilled water are stable without pH adjustment.

Fig. 4 shows the logarithm of viscosity, η of glycerol as a function of shear rate, γ° at different temperatures. The viscosity of glycerol is independent of shear rate, i.e. it is a Newtonian fluid. The viscosity of glycerol at 293.15, 303.15, 313.15, 323.15, and 333.15K was measured as 1149, 491, 237, 125, and 72cP, respectively. The glycerol becomes less viscous with raising temperature so that by each 10°C increase in temperature, the viscosity of glycerol halved. By raising temperature, thermal motions of molecules increase and hence the intramolecular forces weaken.

The logarithm of viscosity of both glycerol and the GQDs–glycerol suspensions with different mass fractions as a function of shear rate (viscosity curve) at 293.15K is shown in Fig. 5 . As this figure shows the viscosity of the suspensions decreases with increasing shear rates at low shear rates (approximately up to 4s−1) and then it reaches a plateau. Hence, at low shear rates, the suspensions behave as non-Newtonian fluids, i.e. their viscosity depends on shear rate. They can be considered as shear-thinning or pseudoplastic fluids. Their shear thinning behavior is more obvious at greater mass fractions so that the suspensions with mass fractions of 0.01 and 0.02 are non-Newtonian fluids up to 6s−1. At higher shear rates, the suspensions are Newtonian fluids. As the shear rate increases, the disarranged molecules orient themselves into the direction of flow and hence the viscosity decreases. On the other hand, the nanoparticles have great tendency to form aggregates because of their high surface energy. As the shear increases the aggregates are broken down into smaller units and hence viscosity decreases.

Fig. 5 also shows the viscosity of the suspensions of GQDs is less than that of glycerol and decreases with increasing the mass fraction at 303.15K. For example, the viscosity of the suspension containing 2wt% of GQDs in glycerol decreases 36.91% compared to that of glycerol at shear rate 6.32−1 and 303.15K. Hence, GQDs act similar as a lubricant when are dispersed in glycerol. In our previous work, we measured the rheological properties of graphene–glycerol suspensions at different mass fractions (0.0025–0.0200) and temperatures (20–60°C) [18]. We showed the viscosity of suspensions enhances with increasing the mass fraction of graphene so that 401.49% enhancement in viscosity of glycerol was attained by loading of 2% graphene nanosheets at shear rate 6.32s−1 and 303.15K. In other words, while the viscosity of glycerol increases by suspending graphene, it decreases by adding GQDs.

Fig. 6 presents the flow curves or the plots of logarithm of shear stress, τ versus shear rate for both glycerol and the GQDs–glycerol suspensions at 313.15K. As this figure shows they flow immediately upon application of stress. The shear stress of the suspensions does not change linearly with shear rate.

Fig. 7 shows the viscosity curves for GQDs–glycerol suspensions with two mass fractions of 0.0025 and 0.0200 at various temperatures. Similar results were obtained for other mass fractions. The shear thinning behavior is more obvious at lower shear rates and temperatures. Due to small size and large surface area of the GQDs, there is a possibility for structuring at low shear rates and a deformation and restructuring at high shear rates. As Fig. 7 shows for all temperatures, the shear thinning property is more noticeable at higher mass fractions. In the nanofluids with low mass fraction of GQDs, the nature of glycerol plays a major role but at higher mass fractions, significant contribution comes from the interaction between the GQDs and glycerol. The viscosity of the suspensions is strongly dependent on temperature so that by each 10°C increase in temperature, the viscosity of the suspensions halved approximately because of weakening the intermolecular forces between the molecules. For example, the viscosity of the nanofluid with mass fraction of 0.02 and shear rate of 3.2s−1 at 20 and 30°C is 625.7924 and 313.6813cP, respectively.

The minimum effective viscosities,
ƞ
nf
min
−
ƞ f
ƞ f
, where ƞ
nf (min) is the minimum nanofluid viscosity and ƞ
f is glycerol viscosity at the same temperature, of the GQDs–glycerol suspensions at different temperatures, shear rates, and mass fractions are summarized in Table 1 . As Table 1 shows, 49.97% reduction in viscosity of glycerol was achieved by loading of 2% GQDs for shear rate of 0.66s−1 at 293.15K.

The TC of GQDs–distilled water suspensions was measured as a function of mass fraction (0.00–0.02) at 298K. As Fig. 8a shows TC of the nanofluids increases linearly with increasing mass fraction. The TC enhancement of nanofluids can be calculated using the following equation:
(3)
TC
enhancement % =
k nf
− k
k
× 100
where κ and κ nf are the thermal conductivity of distilled water and nanofluid, respectively. Fig. 8b shows TC of distilled water increases about 9% when 2% GQDs was loaded at 298K. Hence, GQDs can act as a coolant to dissipate heat from a system. TC of carbonaceous materials at room temperature varies from ~0.01WmK−1 for amorphous carbon to 2000WmK−1 for graphene [25]. The heat in these materials is carried by both electrons and phonons “the quanta of lattice vibrations”. The phonons has the predominate contribution in TC of these materials. The TC of GQDs is less than that graphene due to increased phonon-boundary scattering [25] which decreases the mean free path of the phonons as well as TC.

Fig. 8c shows the TC of GQDs–distilled water suspension with mass fraction of 0.015 at different temperatures. As this figure shows the TC increases sharply with raising temperature. The TC enhancement with temperature can be fitted well (R2 =0.9996) by the following empirical equation:
(4)
k = 04926 + 0.03345
e
0.049
T

The EC, σ of GQDs-distilled water nanofluids (f
m
= 0.0000–0.0004) was measured at different temperatures (25–45°C). The EC of the distilled water was measured as 4μScm−1 at 25°C. As Fig. 9a shows the EC of nanofluid increases with increasing mass fraction and temperature. As temperature increases the viscosity of both distilled water and the nanofluids decreases and electrophoretic mobility which has inverse relationship with viscosity increases. Hence, the EC increases with raising temperature. Fig. 9a shows the EC of distilled water increases by loading GQDs. The FTIR spectrum showed the prepared GQDs are enriched with oxygen functional groups on their surfaces or edges. When the GQDs disperse in water, these groups deprotonate and hence gain negative charge. The negative charge on the surface of GQDs is confirmed by the negative value of zeta potential (Fig. 3b). The formation of surface charge by polarization of GQDs when they are dispersed in distilled water is the main reason for the enhancement of EC.

Fig. 9a also shows the EC of GQDs in distilled water increases linearly with increasing mass fraction so that the EC of the nanofluids can be fitted by following empirical equation:
(5)
σ = α + β
f m
where α and β are temperature-dependent parameters. Table 2 gives the values of these parameters in the studied temperatures with the corresponding correlation coefficients. As Table 2 shows these parameters increase with raising temperature. The parameters can be fitted with the following equations:
(6)
α =
α 0
+ c exp
dT
(7)
β =
β 0
T f
where α 0, c, and d are 24.23μScm−1, 2.016×10−16 μScm−1, and 0.227K−1, respectively. β0 and f are 3.956×104 μScm−1 and 0.064, respectively. The correlation coefficients of Eqs. (5) and (6) are 0.992 and 0.984, respectively.

The EC enhancement of nanofluids can be calculated using the following equation:
(8)
EC
enhancement % =
σ nf
− σ
σ
× 100
where σ and σ nf are the EC of distilled water and nanofluid, respectively. Fig. 8b shows the EC enhancement of the nanofluids increases with increasing mass fraction and decreasing temperature. The maximum EC enhancement for the nanofluid with mass fraction of 0.0004 is around 14,342% at 25°C. The EC of the nanofluids increases with temperature at constant mass fraction while the EC enhancement decreases. In our previous work [19], 18544.44% enhancement in EC of distilled water was obtained by including graphene oxide (GO) with mass fraction of 0.0004 at 25°C. In other words, under similar conditions, GO increases further the EC of distilled water compared with GQDs since it contain more oxygen-containing functional groups.

In this work, we prepared the GQDs with the average particle size of 20nm by one-step green pyrolysis of citric acid. The nanofluids of GQDs were prepared in glycerol and distilled water. The transport properties of the nanofluids including rheology, TC, and EC were measured at various mass fractions of GQDs. This work contains the following main conclusions:
•
The nanofluids of GQDs in distilled water were stable at least for two months. The nanofluid in distilled water had a zeta potential more than −40mV when the pH is equal or greater than to 7. Hence, the nanofluids of GQDs-distilled water are stable without pH adjustment.
•
Both glycerol and the nanofluids of GQDs-glycerol become less viscous with raising temperature.
•
The nanofluids of GQDs-glycerol can be considered as shear-thinning or pseudoplastic fluids at low shear rates and temperatures.
•
The viscosity of glycerol decreases by loading GQDs so that 49.97% reduction in viscosity of glycerol was achieved by loading of 2% GQDs for shear rate of 0.66s−1 at 293.15K.
•
The TC of water increases by adding GQDs and raising temperature. The TC of distilled water increases about 9% when 2% GQDs were loaded at 298K. Also, TC of GQDs- of distilled water increases exponentially with raising temperature.
•
The EC of distilled water increases by loading GQDs through the formation of surface charge by polarization of GQDs in distilled water. The maximum EC enhancement for the nanofluid with mass fraction of 0.0004 is around 14,342% at 25°C.
In summary, this work showed that the nanofluids of GQDs could be used as coolant to dissipate heat from a system and as lubricant to reduce friction and wear.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the Ferdowsi University of Mashhad (grant no: 2/41590) is gratefully acknowledged. The authors would also like to acknowledge Mrs. R. Pesyan and F. Khakrah for taking TEM and AFM images, respectively.
